data.title: Growth And Value: I Am Collecting Big Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Some debates will last for generations, like: Is a hot dog a hot sandwich?

Other debates, though, will be settled through future scientific discoveries. It never ceases to amaze me when we discover a new organ in the human body, an organ that has been present for generations and has only recently been discovered because of the advancements of our scientific ability to look at smaller and smaller pieces.

When it comes to the market, there have been two sides that have often waged an argumentative war against each other. This is the debate on whether it's better to invest in value stocks or invest in growth stocks. Typically, value stocks are those companies that are more mature, with smaller debt levels and larger cash piles. Historically, these are also dividend-paying companies because they reward their shareholders primarily through cash dividends or buybacks.

On the other side of the coin is growth investments. These are investments focused on growing the company, often running with negative cash flow, using cheap debt to fuel their growth. The goal is to become so large that eventually they become cash flow positive. Many of these growth investments either crash and burn into flames or provide massive, stunning returns. Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), and NVIDIA (NVDA) all qualify as historical growth stocks, some of which have turned into what many like to call "elder growth stocks," now that they are massive cash flow producing companies that use debt to fuel their growth.

Today, I want to look at a way to enjoy great income from both growth stocks and value stocks, letting you answer the question: Is it better to invest in growth or invest in value? The answer: if you're looking for great income, I have an option that allows you to collect great income from both sides of the coin.

Let's dive in!

Great Income From Value Stocks

Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), yielding 9.2%, is a CEF that focuses on small-cap stocks. It has consistently outperformed the Russell 2000 for an impressive 35 years. Source

While RVT and the Russell 2000 have joined in the recent market weakness, we expect that small-cap companies will outperform in the big picture. Source

Today, large-cap companies are trading at much higher valuations than small and mid-cap companies. The difference is the widest we have seen since 2000. While small-cap companies did see their valuations decline shortly before the recession in 2001, they dramatically outperformed once the recession started.

From 2000-2004, we saw a period where the S&P 500 was negative, but the Russell 2000 was positive. RVT significantly outperformed both.

We have a very similar starting point today, with large-caps trading at high valuations, while many small to mid-caps are trading at valuations normally seen when recessions are already underway.

RVT has gone through some changes, with founder Chuck Royce taking a step back from the day-to-day management to go into a well-deserved retirement. The management of the fund has been with Royce for a long time, with the "junior" member of the team being at Royce for 9 years. The rest have been with Royce for 17–30 years.

We can be confident that this management team is well-steeped in the Royce culture and are capable of executing in the footsteps of Chuck Royce.

RVT pays a variable dividend, which is calculated based upon the trailing four quarters of NAV. As RVT's NAV goes up and down, the distribution will as well, we provide an estimate of future dividends for the subscribers of High Dividend Opportunities.

RVT is a quick way to invest in small-cap companies, which are trading at very low valuations relative to large-cap companies. Historically, when these large gaps exist, the market eventually closes them. When this occurred in the early '00s, RVT went through a period of significant outperformance. As the valuation gap closes, we believe RVT is a great option to take advantage of that.

Great Income From Growth Stocks

BlackRock Science and Technology Trust, yielding 8.9%, is a CEF focused on Growth investments. NVIDIA and Microsoft are among its top holdings.

BST trades at a persistent discount to NAV, allowing you to gain exposure to growth investments while maximizing value per dollar invested:

This will allow an investor to enjoy exposure to growth investments without deciding when to buy or sell, abdicating that responsibility or that option to the portfolio managers. The real question, do the portfolio managers provide enough value versus the fees they charge to make this a worthwhile consideration, remains.

When we look over the long history of BST, we can see that the portfolio managers have provided exceptional value to their shareholders. While I'm not historically an advocate of selling shares to unlock cash, if you want to use a fund to gain exposure to massive growth investments that do not offer large dividends, something like BST can provide you with the option to do that and not have to worry about the timing of those actions. This is because fund managers are not emotionally invested in the situation will be able to do so from a much more objective position.

BST is likely to see more volatility in the near-term as growth investments see their premium values challenged, but historically, those same investments recover very strongly thereafter. This makes the CEF a good long-term investment through market cycles.

Conclusion

RVT and BST both provide phenomenal recurring income via distributions. They do this by adjusting and trimming their portfolio, collecting the dividends received from their holdings, and recycling capital into new investments. Both of these funds are very actively managed, meaning that the managers make decisions based upon their evaluation of the market and the companies in their holdings. These decisions can either make the fund more successful or make the fund lag in its performance. When compared to the market, we can see that both of these funds have a strong history of outperforming other options – including passive market ETFs (SPY). We can determine that these management teams provide true additional value to their shareholders. I'm not a big fan of having to trade in and out of holdings to try and unlock cash to live off of; however, I am willing to leverage the skills of others who have a positive track record doing that to pay me.

Retirement should be a time where you expend a ton of energy doing the things you were not able to do during your working life. For many, that means visiting family, traveling to see national parks, exploring other parts of the globe, or simply sitting at home in front of your fireplace with a hot cup of coffee and spending time with the ones you love the most. At the end of the day, most of us in our retirements don't want to be spending it chasing after different investments in the market or trying to time the market to unlock as much cash from each investment as possible. Should you invest in value or invest in growth for your retirement's income? The answer is both – by using these two funds, you can unlock income from both sides of the coin. Regardless of whether the economy is booming or busting, one of these two funds will always be consistently providing you with great income, if not both of them at the same time.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Why Multi-Theme Investing? Why GXDW?
data.description:
We’re living in a golden age of disruption, and thematic investing has become an indispensable tool for investors that seek to capitalize on structural change. By design, thematic strategies target long-term shifts, but their performance is often shaped by macroeconomic forces such as inflation, fiscal policy, capital cycles, interest rates, inventory cycles, or even geopolitics. While these factors may not alter the direction of the structural trend, they might often dictate short-term appetite for the strategy.

Multi-theme ETFs seek to simplify thematic investing by bundling multiple high-potential trends into a single strategy. But we believe a successful theme construction approach should go beyond backward-looking metrics like sales growth or earnings estimates to identify true disruptors. Moreover, a diversified basket of disruptive themes without the flexibility to adapt with the market’s direction can be self-defeating, offsetting winners with laggards and ultimately undermining performance.

In constructing multi-theme solutions, we believe a more dynamic approach is needed—one that captures emerging leadership as themes gain traction. We believe momentum indicators provide a disciplined framework for balancing market timing with thematic conviction. That’s why we have revamped our multi-theme offering, the Global X Thematic Growth ETF (previously traded under the ticker GXTG), and renaming it the Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW). GXDW seeks to track the Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Thematic RotationTM Total Return Index, which leverages Dorsey Wright’s proprietary Relative Strength methodology and dynamically rotates into the five themes with the strongest momentum across Global X’s Thematic ETF universe, offering investors a more agile way to access multiple themes through a single investment.

Key Takeaways

  • Disruption is accelerating, making thematic investing an essential tool for investors to stay agile and rapidly adapt.
  • Multi-theme strategies offer the power of thematic investing in a single strategy but need to evolve to balance disruptive potential with market reality.
  • Rotating into themes with higher momentum may be a more effective solution for multi-theme construction than earnings estimates or backward-looking metrics like sales growth.

Change Is the Only Constant

The modern economy is defined by accelerating disruption. Technology, demographic shifts, resource scarcity, and emerging forces like deglobalization are reshaping entire industries, while creating new investment opportunities. Most importantly, these structural shifts are converging in a way that amplifies their impact. AI is reshaping sectors like Healthcare, Financial Services, or even Media, energy is colliding with technology, and the digital and physical worlds are fusing, accelerating adoption curves and compelling corporations to rethink their investments. Innovation has never been linear, but today, its scale and speed are unprecedented.

Thematic investing offers a strategic approach that seeks to capitalize on these changes, isolating high-growth trends that may define the future. Ideally, thematic investing follows an unconstrained approach to capturing innovation, ignoring geographic boundaries, style biases, or traditional sector limitations. Market capitalization or size of a company should also not be restrictive factors. This broad, unconstrained approach can help gain exposure to the fastest growing and most rapidly adopted technologies.

Over the last 15 years, Global X has identified several of these powerful themes and developed targeted ETFs to provide investors with efficient access to companies benefitting from disruption. With the latest launches of the Global X U.S. Electrification ETF (ZAP), Global X Infrastructure Development ex-U.S. ETF (IPAV), and the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD), we now offer 32 ETFs that target high conviction, investable, long-term themes.1 At the industry level, U.S.-listed thematic ETFs managed nearly $90 billion in assets as of December 2024, a nearly fivefold increase over the past five years, which underscores the growing role of thematic strategies.2

But with over thirty mature themes recognized by Global X alone, and nearly 280 thematic ETFs available to investors in the U.S., in addition to other thematic solutions, selecting the right themes at the right time is becoming an increasingly arduous task.3

Simplifying Theme Selection

Multi-theme ETFs help investors navigate an increasingly crowded thematic landscape by providing exposure to multiple high-growth trends within a single investment. Their diversified structure not only simplifies theme selection but may also mitigates concentration risk in particular geographies, sectors, and market capitalizations. However, the effectiveness of these ETFs ultimately depends on how they are constructed.

We believe a key weakness in many multi-theme ETFs is their reliance on backward-looking metrics—such as sales growth or earnings—to select themes. While these fundamentals offer insight into long-term potential, they fail to capture shifting market dynamics or macroeconomic forces. Additionally, sales or earnings growth data typically emerges quarterly or semi-annually, while markets react far more swiftly. This lag may leave multi-theme ETFs exposed to themes facing short-term headwinds, potentially undermining performance.

Not All Themes Are Created Equal

While many themes are designed keeping a large total addressable market opportunity in mind, macroeconomic forces such as interest rates, fiscal policy, monetary policy, regulation, inflation, or even consumer behavior shifts can significantly influence the short-term trajectory of thematic trends, amplifying or dampening their momentum.

For instance, some themes like Electric Vehicles or Clean Technologies may require significant capital to become viable, meaning performance can lag in periods with high interest rates as the cost of funding innovation goes up. Similarly, themes like AI or Data Centers, reliant on large scale infrastructure investment, tend to favor well-capitalized incumbents with existing technological and computing advantages in early stages. Commodity-driven themes, such as Lithium, are highly sensitive to inventory cycles and fluctuating commodity prices. Themes such as Uranium and Nuclear Power can be limited by permitting cycles and regulatory factors.

Additionally, themes generally progress through distinct adoption cycles, often defined by the S-shaped adoption curve.4 Early stages of adoption are marked by experimentation and skepticism, while later stages see widespread integration, but also saturation risks. As a result, assessing a theme’s current positioning requires more than recognizing its disruptive potential or its immediate fundamental trajectory, and demands a deep understanding and recognition of market conditions and timing.

We believe investors should be selective, prioritizing themes that not only drive innovation but also align with favorable macroeconomic tailwinds. In our view, a multi-theme ETF that fails to account for these factors risks undermining long-term performance.

Balancing Innovation with Market Reality

The market constantly adapts, pricing in new realities. We believe well-constructed multi-theme solutions should in fact seek to leverage market movements to their advantage to understand what themes are positioned for outperformance relative to others.

Research suggests that investing when the market is scaling new heights is an effective long-term strategy, as strength often signals potential for outperformance.5 Winning themes tend to continue winning, further benefiting from capital inflows, increasing adoption, and strengthening narratives.6

Moreover, by systematically rotating into stronger themes and reducing exposure to relative underperformers, a market momentum-based strategy can exit poorly performing strategies, potentially helping the portfolio remain resilient during market downturns, which is key to the long-term success of a multi-theme ETF solution—where wild swings aren’t uncommon.

Multi-Theme Solution with Momentum at the Core

Ultimately, a superior multi-theme solution must balance disruptive potential with real-world market forces. With that framework in mind, we are revamping our multi-theme offering, transitioning from the Global X Thematic Growth ETF (GXTG) to the Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW), placing market momentum at the center of theme selection.

How GXDW Works

GXDW tracks the Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Thematic Rotation Total Return Index (NDWTR), which selects five thematic ETFs that demonstrate the strongest relative momentum in the markets. To qualify for inclusion, an ETF must be sponsored by Global X and focus on a specific, structurally disruptive theme or have a significant overweight toward a particular theme. Currently, the starting universe consists of 27 ETFs.7

To construct the index, Nasdaq Dorsey Wright applies its proprietary Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Relative Strength Matrix methodology, which seeks to rank ETFs in the starting universe based on their relative price momentum. Nasdaq Dorsey Wright uses Point and Figure charting to record supply and demand trends, filtering for insignificant trends and short-term market noise, focusing on capturing long-term price movements in the underlying ETFs. The top five ETFs with the strongest momentum are included in the index, seeking to track themes positioned for outperformance.

Key Methodology Highlights

  • Starting Universe: The starting universe consists of 27 thematic ETFs sponsored by Global X, which target a specific theme or that has a significant overweight toward a particular theme.
  • Momentum-Driven Selection: The Nasdaq Dorsey Wright method assigns a relative strength rank to each ETF in the starting universe, identifying the top five ranked ETFs for final inclusion in the index.
  • Equal Weighting: Selected ETFs receive an equal starting weight, providing balanced exposure to the most promising themes.
  • Rebalancing & Reconstitution: The index is evaluated monthly, with weighting adjustments made only when themes are added or removed, with the objective of allowing trends to develop without frequent turnover.

Momentum is a time-tested factor in investing. By focusing on sustained market strength while filtering out short-term fluctuations, GXDW provides investors with exposure to high-growth themes potentially poised for long-term outperformance.

Conclusion: GXDW Is a Multi-Theme Solution that Seeks to Harness Momentum

Thematic investing offers a powerful way to tap into structural changes reshaping the economy, but success lies in identifying the right themes at the right times. The Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW) leverages a momentum-based approach in an effort to dynamically capture the most promising disruptive trends, seeking to align investors with long-term growth drivers. By focusing on sustained market momentum, this strategy can help investors navigate the complexities of thematic investing, providing a disciplined, data-driven path to potentially capitalize on the next wave of transformative growth.

Related ETFs

GXDW – Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF

2Global X ETFs based on data from Bloomberg. (n.d.). Accessed on January 30, 2025.

3Global X ETFs analysis based on data from Bloomberg. (n.d.). Accessed on January 30, 2025.

4Science Direct. (n.d.) Diffusion of Innovation, by E.M. Rogers. Accessed on February 27, 2025.

data.title: Time To Leave U.S. Treasuries For The Duration?
data.description:
By Robin Marshall, Director, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

We examine why US spreads haven’t tightened during recent Fed easing and why the WGBI outperformed the WGBI-x US, when the Fed raised rates, perhaps because of Treasuries’ shorter duration and convexity. But that may be a disadvantage if duration becomes the investor’s friend in 2025-26.

Greater global divergence in growth, inflation and debt levels has delivered less predictable sovereign spreads in major markets. For example, US Treasury spreads versus the WGBI (EX.US) generally moved procyclically after the GFC, rising during economic upswings, when the Fed generally tightened policy first, and more rapidly, and then falling sharply in downswings or after deflationary shocks, like the GFC and Covid, when the Fed usually led easing and tightening cycles. But US spreads haven’t declined overall in the easing cycle to date, they are close to the levels when the Fed first cut rates in September 2024, or a little wider, as Chart 1 shows, even after the recent mini-flight to quality on tariff uncertainty.

Chart 1: US sovereign spreads since 2018

Multiple factors contributing to spreads…

Stronger US economic growth, sticky inflation and “higher for longer” US rates may have been key factors in the recent spread widening. But another key driver could be the US enacting tax cuts in 2017-18, followed by infrastructure spending. Foreign holdings of US Treasuries have also fallen sharply as a share of the total since 2013, with the main reduction in foreign official holdings, notably central bank holdings, which fell from nearly 25% of the total to only 12%, and not private sector holdings, which remain relatively stable, as Chart 2 shows. Private foreign holdings of Treasuries have not increased to offset the decline in official holdings, perhaps because the inverted US yield curve and increased dollar fx hedging costs effectively removed the yield advantage in Treasuries since 2022.[1]

Chart 2: US Federal debt and holdings since 2000

While other G7 governments also provided Covid fiscal support, the US’s policy decisions pre-Covid resulted in increased government debt levels prior to G7 peers. As a result, US federal debt has roughly doubled since 2016, and the US debt/GDP ratio now exceeds other G7 members, apart from Japan, as Chart 3 shows.

Chart 3: G7 and China Debt/GDP ratios

US term premium has increased despite lower inflation…

Perhaps unsurprisingly, this has also led to an increase in the US term premium, on 10 yr Treasuries, to the highest levels since 2011. Chart 4 shows that although the initial increase in the term premium after Covid was correlated with higher inflation rates, the increase has continued, despite the decline in inflation since 2022. Higher fiscal deficits, govt bond issuance and steadily rising US government debt/GDP ratios may explain the increased US term premium, as markets re-assessed fiscal policy plans, government debt issuance, and medium-term inflation risks.

Chart 4: US term premium on 10 yr Treasury and inflation

We also note that the US has a substantial WGBI weighting of nearly 43%, dwarfing other markets, as Table 1 shows.

Table 1: FTSE World govt bond index weights.

WGBI constituents US European govt bond index All others Japan UK WGBI
Weight in index % 42.8 26.2 16.1 10.1 4.7 100.0

Source: FTSE Russell, Feb.28, 2025 data. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Despite these factors, WGBI EXC.US under-performed WGBI

But despite the high US weight in the WGBI, US sovereign spreads not narrowing since the Fed began easing rates in September 2024, the Fed’s slower than expected easing of interest rates, and the higher US term premium, the WGBI EXC.US has still underperformed the WGBI, since 2020, as Chart 5 shows. This shows the underperformance occurs in both local currency and US dollar terms, so this is not a currency effect alone. Note that the most significant period of under-performance was during the period of rising rates and yields in 2022-23.

Chart 5: Performance of WGBI and WGBI (EXC.US)

Why has the WGBI exc. US underperformed the WGBI?

Why is this? The best explanation for this may be duration[2], since US Treasury market duration has fallen relative to other major WGBI constituencies since Covid, not only because of higher US yields but also the switch to even shorter US issuance. Chart 6 shows how the modified duration of the WGBI EX.US has increased relative to the WGBI over the last 20 yrs, and particularly after the GFC in 2008-09 and Covid in 2020, when the US Treasury issued a larger proportion of shorter US Treasuries.

Chart 6: Duration of WGBI and WGBI ex-US since 2005

Is duration becoming the investor’s friend again?

Chart 7 shows that the US Treasury market has significantly shorter average duration in both conventional and inflation-linked bonds than other major markets. Indeed, none of the major markets have a shorter duration than the US. This was an advantage during the period of Fed tightening and rising yields in 2022-23, given the lower sensitivity of prices to higher yields in the Treasury market than other major WGBI constituents. Now, however, as central banks have entered easing cycles, the shorter duration in US Treasuries may be less of an advantage, since if yields do fall, prices will increase less in the US than in Europe or the UK.

Chart 7: Average duration of major govt & inflation-linked markets

Similar considerations apply to convexity – higher outside the US

Extending the analysis to convexity, we find similar results. Remember that the convexity[3] of a bond measures the change in its duration for a change in its yield. So, a bond with positive convexity will increase in duration as yields decline, giving the investor higher returns, and smaller losses when yields rise, since duration falls as yields increase. This makes higher positive convexity in bonds desirable for an investor, ceteris paribus. Chart 8 shows the convexity of the WGBI, and WGBI ex-US, since 2020, and the higher convexity in the WGBI ex-US.

Chart 8: Convexity of WGBI and WGBI ex-US since Covid

"Higher for longer" to reinforce impact of lower US duration?

Another factor here is that the US Federal Reserve may continue slower easing than other central banks, and hold rates higher for longer, particularly if US tax cuts are enacted and proposed tariff increases boost US aggregate demand and inflation. Although tariff increases may extend to Europe, weaker growth and inflation give the Eurozone more room to ease rates faster than the US.

But even if the Fed can ease policy faster than expected and exceeds the 50bp easing discounted for the rest of 2025, the extra duration and convexity in Eurozone and UK government bonds would allow them to at least match US Treasury gains.

Footnotes

1. See Foreign Institutional Investors, Monetary Policy, And Reaching For Yield BIS Working Papers No 1153, 11 December 2023

2. Duration measures a bond’s sensitivity to a rise in yield. This can be measured by using the weighted cash flows or Macaulay duration, or the modified duration, which adjusts Macaulay duration for changing yields to maturity.

3. Mathematically, convexity is the 2nd derivative of the bond’s price with respect to a change in yield, where duration is the 1st derivative

data.title: RWE: At An Inflection Point After Activist Investor Stake
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome DPRG Investment Management as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

RWEOY stock rallied about 15% in the 45 days leading up to the company's 4Q24 earnings report, likely driven by Elliot Advisors accumulating its position. Shortly after, the activist investor group announced a 5% stake in the company.

Investment Thesis

RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY) (OTC:RWNEF) (OTCPK:RWNFF) is at an inflection point, following the Elliott Advisors' 5% stake in the company. The involvement of the activist investor signals a potential shift toward stricter capital discipline and shareholder-friendly measures which can boost shareholder value.

Reduced CAPEX and increased share buybacks could lead to meaningful multiple expansion, with investors willing to accept higher than historical-average multiples for RWE. The share's immediate positive reaction to Elliott's stake, reflected in the stock's recent rally, further supports the thesis. Furthermore, recent moves by RWE's management suggest a high likelihood of alignment with the proposed measures.

From a fundamentals point of view, RWEOY appears undervalued. The company’s robust financial position and profitability compared to industry medians underscore the upside potential. Our valuation incorporates two scenarios: one where management fully embraces Elliott's proposal and one where it does not.

Company Overview

Revenue Breakdown & Growth Drivers

RWE AG is the biggest utility company in Germany with a strong focus on renewable and climate-friendly power generation. It operates primarily in Europe and the U.S.

RWE operates through five primary business segments:

  • Offshore Wind (5%) – Develops and operates offshore wind farms, generating revenue from electricity sales.
  • Onshore Wind & Solar (10%) – Manages wind, solar, and battery storage projects.
  • Flexible Generation (5%) – Includes hydro, biomass, and gas-fired power plants.
  • Supply & Trading (81%) – A highly profitable segment focused on energy commodity trading, risk management, and long-term supply agreements.
  • Phaseout Technologies (3%) – Manages lignite mining and nuclear decommissioning, benefiting from government compensation for coal phaseouts.

RWE is also actively expanding into hydrogen production, battery storage, and hydrogen-ready gas plants to pursue its aggressive renewable energy transition goals.

Its energy trading division is the key profit driver for RWE, leveraging market optimization and risk management strategies to enhance returns.

Focus On Renewable Energy: Strategy & Innovation

RWE's strategy outlined in the annual report highlights the company's focus on the green energy transition. Below are key highlights:

  1. Transition from traditional fossil fuels to green energy sources like wind, solar and hydro.
  2. Invest in battery storage and flexible backup power plants, including hydrogen-ready gas-fired stations.
  3. Expand green energy capacity to support growing power needs.
  4. Ramp up hydrogen innovation and infrastructure.
  5. Commitment to carbon-neutral operations: aiming for Paris Agreement alignment, translating into reducing Scope 1 & 2 emissions by 65% and Scope 3 emissions by 42%, by 2030.
  6. Initial commitment of €55 billion in net green investments between 2024-2030, now revised to €35 billion between 2025-2030 (through "Growing Green" program.)

The company's green energy strategy is underscored by its renewable energy Research and Development (R&D) efforts.

  • Research on optimal wind farm placement for energy loss minimization and advanced wind speed analysis techniques, setting new industry standards.
  • Use of drones to deliver repair parts and tools to offshore wind farms, reducing downtimes.
  • Use of large-scale batteries (7.5 MW) for grid stabilization and improved electricity dispatchability.
  • Large-scale hydrogen storage and production projects including a 300MW electrolyzer and building Germany's first commercial hydrogen cavern storage.
  • Carbon capture and recycling technologies, achieving up to 99% CO2 recovery and potential for industrial scale closed carbon cycles.

Global Operations Breakdown

The company's biggest market is Germany, accounting for about half (46% of Total Revenue) of the company's top line. RWE is meaningfully invested in the rest of Europe (24%), while the UK stands as its second-largest individual country (22%). Lastly, North America accounted for just 6% of total revenues in 2024, while other regions remain non-material at 1%.

Table 1: RWE Regional Revenue and Intangible Assets & PPE per region according to the latest annual report.

Regional Revenue (EUR, mil.) Germany UK Rest of Europe North America Other
Revenue 11,217 5,315 5,784 1,567 341

Renewable Power Production Regional Breakdown

Renewable power generation is a key segment for RWE going forward, as outlined in the "Focus On Renewable Energy: Strategy & Innovation" section. Analyzing this segment provides color about the company's focus, strengths and future direction.

Onshore Wind is the largest power producing segment for RWE (43% of total power production in 2024) with the US accounting for the majority (61%) of the wind energy produced, and a broad wind farm portfolio across Europe. Solar Energy dethroned Offshore Wind in 2024 as the second-biggest power producing segment, accounting for 24% of the total output of power, 88% of which comes from US solar farms.

Finally, Hydro and Biomass are less impactful, with 5% and 6% of the total power production in 2024. The UK accounts for the majority of Offshore Wind, while Germany and the Netherlands account for the majority of Hydro and Biomass power production respectively.

The big takeaway is RWE's significant investment in renewable energy projects in the US, particularly in Onshore Wind and Solar Energy.

Financial Position

RWE maintains a healthy financial position.

It is well capitalized, with a current ratio of 1.27, indicating sufficient short-term assets. Its leverage appears well-balanced with a 0.43 debt-to-equity ratio combined with a financial debt-to-EBITDA of 0.47. Finally, the company's interest coverage ratio of 80.8 demonstrates strong solvency, with interest payments on its debt well covered by operating income.

The Catalyst

Activist Shareholder Stake Can Boost Margins And Share Buybacks

The following chart demonstrates the disconnect between improving operating cash flow and free cash flow, with a surging CAPEX to Revenue ratio. RWE's heavy CAPEX spending, directed primarily to renewable energy projects, has been weighing on its cash flows and margins.

Following a profitability warning for 2025 (see "A Tough 2025 Is Not A Dealbreaker"), the company announced a €10 billion (25%) cut of planned investments through 2030. The announcement was generally praised by investors, including Elliott Advisors, who advocate for further capital discipline and share buyback expansion.

We believe that due to this, management will likely align with the activist investor's suggestions, given the ongoing share buyback program and 4Q24 cost-cutting initiatives.

According to the company’s latest report on March 25, RWE purchased 35,000 shares (worth ~€1.2 million) in the trading days between 17th and 21st of March, with a total of ~8 million shares bought between its announcement in November 2024 to date. This acts as a positive sign that the company intends to fully implement its share buyback program and potentially increase it further soon.

Activist Investor Pressure Works

Elliott Advisors has a strong track record of driving shareholder value in similar situations.

In November 2024, Elliott acquired a 5% stake in Tokyo Gas (OTCPK:TKGSY) making it one of the biggest shareholders. They pushed the Japanese utility company to monetize its considerable real estate portfolio which at the time was valued around all of its market capitalization at ~$9B. Since then, the stock has risen more than 30%.

In June 2018, Elliott and Bluescape Resources proposed a plan to restructure Sempra Energy (SRE) by separating it into an LNG and midstream enterprise and a pure-play regulated utility. In the following 18 months leading up to the COVID-19 crash, the stock rose more than 40%. (Note: the fall in the TTM (trailing twelve-month) multiples in the chart below is attributed to improved financial performance rather than deteriorating multiples.)

Given Elliott's proven success in unlocking shareholder value in similar scenarios, we believe the multiple expansion argument laid out in the "Investment Thesis" is well-supported.

A Tough 2025 Is Not A Dealbreaker

According to the latest 4Q24 earnings call, RWE management expects 2025 to be a tough year due to earnings normalization in Flexible Generating & Trading segments. This is largely attributed due to the normalization of historically high and volatile power prices which benefited the company over the last few years.

On a positive note, this dip is expected to be temporary with RWE management expecting its Flexible Generation and Trading segments to recover at a 6% CAGR from 2025-2027 with an EBITDA target range of €1.1 billion to €1.6 billion by 2027.

Sector & Peer Group Comparison

RWE competes with major European utilities like E.ON (OTCPK:ENAKF), Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY), Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDSF), Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY), and Ørsted (DNNGY), as well as global renewable players such as NextEra Energy (NEE) and Brookfield Renewable (BEP). The company’s is one of the most aggressive players in developing its renewable energy pipeline and has developed and advanced its energy trading capabilities. It is also geographically diversified across Europe, the U.S., and Asia-Pacific.

Below, we analyze RWE's profitability, valuation and growth metrics, compared to industry medians. Also, the company's metrics are compared with E.ON and Iberdrola which, we believe, are the most relevant competitors due to their similar geographic and operational exposure, as well as their focus on the renewable energy transition. It is worth noting that RWE holds a 15% stake in E.ON.

Profitability Sector And Peer Group Evaluation

From a profitability perspective, RWE appears attractive, with a Seeking Alpha score of 'A'.

Specifically, the company boasts appealing operating margins (TTM EBIT Margin: 28% vs. sector median of 22%), with solid return on equity (TTM Return on Equity: 16% vs. sector median of 9%) and bottom-line margins (TTM Net Income Margin: 21% vs. sector median of 13%.) CAPEX/Sales is also worth noting, with a +9.18% difference from the industry median, demonstrating some room for cost-cutting measures.

Over the last three years, RWE has shown meaningful Operating Margin and Profit Margin expansion from ~10% to ~31% and ~8% to ~21% respectively, outpacing E.ON and Iberdrola. However, Return on Equity has not improved as much, due to higher capital investment and an expanding asset base.

Valuation Peer Evaluation

On the valuation side, RWEOY's 'A-' grade demonstrates the appeal of the stock as a value play. Key valuation metrics include FWD EV/Sales: 1.24x (vs. sector median of 4.32x), FWD EV/EBITDA: 4.04x (vs. sector median of 11.12x) and FWD P/E Non-GAAP: 15.60x (vs. sector median of 18.18x).

The mid-term outlook for the company is also solid, with a forecasted EPS CAGR of 18% from 2025-2027, reaching 3 EUR per share.

On a TTM (trailing-twelve-month) basis, the EV/EBITDA and PE ratios demonstrate RWE's room for multiple expansion and RWE's relative undervaluation for most of the lookback period.

Growth Peer Evaluation

RWE’s sluggish growth metrics receive an ‘F’ grade, which is attributed to a confluence of factors.

First, due to lower wholesale electricity prices which declined considerably from the elevated levels seen in 2022-2023 following the war in Ukraine.

Second, due to reduced thermal generation volumes (lignite) which is part of RWE’s decarbonization strategy.

Finally, reduced volatility in the energy trading markets resulted in lower returns for its Supply & Trading segment.

When comparing to E.ON and Iberdrola, the correlation is evident. The revenue slowdown was expected, following two extraordinary years (2022-2023) for European utility stocks, particularly due to historically high-energy prices. EBITDA and Net Income demonstrated greater variability due to company-specific operational exposures and one-time effects.

Valuation

To incorporate both our bullish and conservative estimates into a single price target, we use a Bullish Target and a Base Target, which are then weighted according to our estimated probability of each scenario occurring. This approach allows our readers to examine our assumptions and adjust the target price according to their own views.

We assign a 70% probability to the bullish scenario, considering the management's recent actions (share buybacks and 4Q24 cost-cutting initiatives), the stock price appreciation following the stake announcement, and the successful track record of Elliott in similar activist scenarios.

Our Forward Financial Metrics (Table 2, 3) are based on the next twelve-month estimates for Revenue, EBITDA and EPS provided by Bloomberg. Each scenario uses the average of EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA and P/E to reach a target price.

Bullish & Conservative Target Multiples Selection

Anchoring target multiples in realistic expectations is important. To do this, we look at the 5-year history of Blended Forward multiples for RWEOY stock.

Our bullish scenario includes target multiples of 1.8x EV/Revenue, 8.0x EV/EBITDA, and 20.0x P/E, which were achieved during 2022-23 when surging energy prices benefitted the company. We believe that these levels could become the new baseline going forward, given the outlined bullish scenario.

For our conservative scenario, we use 1.3x EV/Revenue, 7.0x EV/EBITDA and 15.0x P/E, aligned with the 5-year mean multiples.

Bullish Valuation

If management implements Elliott’s proposed measures, including reducing CAPEX, improving capital allocation and boosting shareholder returns through buybacks and dividends, we think the stock will return to multiples seen in 2022, around one standard deviation above its 5-year mean multiples.

Conservative/Base Valuation

If the status quo is maintained, without significant cost-cutting measures or increases in share buybacks, we expect RWEOY to trade near its 5-year historical mean multiples.

Action Recommendation

Our Average Blended Target Price of ~$46 represents a +28% from current levels (~$36), indicating moderate upside in the shot-term. Given the recent share price appreciation, we suggest waiting for a better entry point for new investors around $31, where the stock has demonstrated a strong technical support. At this price, the risk/reward becomes more compelling, with +48% upside from our target price of $46. Potential investors should monitor the price movements and potential pullbacks for optimal entry.

Risks

RWE’s ambitious renewable energy plans mean the company needs to out-invest its competitors, which hurts profitability and cashflows in the short term, combined with uncertainty related to the timing and magnitude of such long-term investments.

Due to the ‘Green Premium’ (i.e. the marginal cost of generating green energy vs. its traditional alternative), RWE relies on government incentives and regulation to successfully develop a profitable renewable energy asset portfolio without materially impacting its balance sheet and profitability in the process.

Persistently low-energy commodity prices can hurt the company, which relies on its trading segment for a significant portion of its profitability. Although the company has projected a gradual recovery from 2026 onwards, macro factors could push the recovery story down the line.

If RWE's management elects to resist activist investor demands, the share price could face an eventual selling pressure, with Elliott offloading their stake.

Conclusion

RWEOY is at a pivotal moment, following the involvement of activist investor's Elliott Management 5% stake and its push for cost-cutting measures and increased share buybacks. RWE's management already showed signs of alignment with these proposals through cost-cutting announcements in 4Q24 and the continuation of its share buyback program. The rise of the share price, and relative stability following the activist stake announcement, demonstrates that other investors are also aligned with Elliott's proposals.

The company's strong profitability and attractive valuation metrics, when compared to industry peers, underpin the RWE investment thesis. Sluggish growth in 2025 is expected and temporary, due to announced divestments and energy price normalization. If Elliott measures are implemented, additional buying support could materialize from investors looking to benefit from this shareholder-friendly strategy transition.

Our thesis employs a binary valuation approach, incorporating a bullish and conservative scenario, yielding a blended price target of ~$46 (+28% upside from $36).

Due to the recent share increase and limited upside for new entrants, we initiate RWEOY with a 'HOLD' rating. We suggest investors to monitor the stock and capitalize on share price weakness around $31 where the risk/reward profile is enhanced through a technical support and enhanced potential returns (+48% upside to $46).

data.title: Don't Mind The Turbulence, These Airline Stocks Are Scheduled For Takeoff
data.description:
The last month has been a nightmare for airline investors, with many headlines and macroeconomic headwinds bringing the 2024 rallies crashing down. But who are the potential winners of this dip, and why could it be just a dip and not a sign of a sustained downturn? Let's do a crash course on airline valuation.

Why have airlines been so bearish recently?

Since August 2024, JETS (Global Airline Index) has seen an astronomical rally of over 58% responding to airlines, on average, recovering to profitability after the pandemic and foot traffic rising to pre-pandemic levels with bullish travel forecast for the following 6 months, until the 20th of January. What happened on the 20th of January? The Inauguration of Donald J. Trump to the Office of the President of the United States of America.

In his speech, he pledged a dedication to tariffs attempting to bring jobs back to America. This caused economic turmoil across the markets. The first stocks to be hit by this type of turmoil are airlines, as they are heavily exposed to macroeconomic trends. If the average person has less money, they will fly less. This turmoil then prolonged for months, plunging the S&P 500 into correction territory and threatening a US recession, with the risk of the US being cut out of the global supply chain.

In addition to multiple high-profile air accidents around the world causing distress, coupled with Boeing's controversies, the gutting of the FAA, and a fire at Heathrow, the airline industry has experienced no moments of calm. Domestic US airlines are reporting a reduced outlook for 2025, studies show the tariff war is impacting transatlantic travel, Canadian-American flight outlooks are down over 70%, and now Virgin Atlantic reports lower foot traffic. This is a very fearful time for airline investors.

Where is the opportunity?

The opportunity lies in efficiency. When economies of scale don't work, airlines will have to be as efficient as possible to maintain profits and survive a dip in travel. Long-term: there is an increasing global population, with millions being lifted out of poverty (from a global perspective) every month, and international travel becoming cheaper as planes become more fuel-efficient and oil prices drop. The airlines that best weather the storm could be set up for a large return when foot traffic eventually recovers.

We can find which Airlines are the most likely candidates by looking at multiple valuation and profitability ratios compared to the industry average and their own historic average. In addition, we should account for the risks the airline is exposed to and the markets/routes they frequent. Data for this analysis was taken from the IATA for industry averages, each company's respective FY2024 reports, and the Seeking Alpha page. The chosen airlines are mainstream with positive earnings and a fleet size of at least 100 aircraft.

The most major ratio we can use for airlines is TRATK - Total Revenue per Available Tonne Kilometer which shows us the relationship between the revenue of the airline and the available seat kilometers over a year which is a practical return on the aggregate airline tickets available to the public showing its real sales efficiency and against the industry.

Price ratios provide a basic valuation compared to the industry average. To calculate a basic ROA, we can determine the following, with the mean reflecting the efficiency coefficient ranking of each airline against the industry average per fleet, and the upside indicating the ranking per aircraft.

Before we rank our airlines, we have to consider market exposure. Each market has contributed to industry YoY RPK growth this year, except for North America. February does show a significant slowdown globally, however. Ranking each market against the IATA data below can give us a market rating.

Despite all this, however, YoY, The industry as a whole is getting more efficient by load factor, showing the ability to fill the planes is improving.

By incorporating all of this data into a ranking system, we can see which Airlines are the best set up for the coming years to take advantage of the current markets, and which are the most value for money for investors.

Some takeaways

There are major risks involved with airlines, mainly macroeconomic, so one should be well in the loop when investing in airlines to prevent exposure to large losses. Even though the financial valuations may look enticing, historically airline PE ratios are rarely above 15.

LATAM, ranked number 1 against our system, is a promising option for a speculative grower off the back of this airline dip, seeing as it has very high efficiency, but it is relatively new and has a smaller fleet and sector growth than other airlines. So any growth will most likely be smaller but safer.

IAG being the number 1 in Europe, is a rapidly growing and most efficient airline in Europe, which has a vast repertoire of routes, domestic and international, diversifying its revenue streams, but also its risk. This is slightly mitigated by its severe undervaluation.

Delta is the number 1 US airline against our system and number 3 overall, this reflects the record year FY2024 was for Delta, with high efficiency meaning they are the most appropriate airline to weather the US domestic airline storm, with a nice tailwind from undervaluation to battle the tariff fears.

Cathay is number 4 and Asia's top airline, which shows its strong 2024 growth in the strongest growing market, beefing its rating despite its lackluster efficiency growth YoY.

This ranking gives us an idea of the best-rated airlines with the widest FCF margins against their price, the most efficient sales per seat kilometer, and the lowest PEG. These properties will give the airline the ability to properly navigate demand reduction with a margin to absorb losses.

All of these, of course, should be researched individually and put in the context of the market outlook at large, for example, this deep dive valuation of IAG I did earlier in the year, but I am quietly confident that the foot traffic will bounce back globally, and will not hopefully not deteriorate much further referring to the IATA load factor figures.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: New 52-Week Low, Near 12% Yield Attractive Amidst Rising Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

With the market experiencing volatility from what seems like new tariffs implemented every other day, investors are seemingly cautious when it comes to investing, and rightfully so.

As an income investor, my portfolio has held up well as I'm heavily weighted towards blue-chip dividend stocks with reliable cash flows who will likely continue paying me steady income.

And while NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP) isn't a blue-chip stock by definition, their double-digit yield and solid fundamentals are attractive for income-oriented investors.

This riskier REIT recently hit a new 52-week low of $14.20, signaling a bottom may be in and now may be a good time to accumulate shares.

While they may experience further volatility in the future due to uncertainty surrounding the cannabis sector, they remain a buy for income-oriented investors & retirees.

In this article, I discuss their latest performance, fundamentals, and why I think NewLake Capital's near 12% yield deserves a spot in your portfolio.

Previous Buy Case

I last covered NewLake Capital with a buy rating despite the stock's share price declining. Since then, their volatility has continued, with the stock down nearly 12% compared to the S&P's 6.25%.

This, in my opinion, has less to do with the REIT's fundamentals and more to do with economic & cannabis scheduling uncertainty.

NLCP also has exposure to troubled tenant PharmaCann, but this is minimal in comparison to peer Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR).

And while their issues linger, NewLake's price-to-book value of 0.84x, conservative AFFO payout ratio, and low-leveraged balance sheet made them a better buy compared to IIPR.

Tenant Headwinds Linger

NewLake Capital reported their Q4 earnings in early March with what was a lackluster performance due to two troubled tenants. FFO was in-line with analysts' estimates at $0.47 while revenue saw a slight decline from the previous quarter.

This amounted to $12.5 million, down from $12.55 million, while FFO retracted $0.02 over the same period. Year-over-year FFO was down $0.04 while their top line declined from roughly $13 million due to non-cash items regarding Revolutionary Clinics.

With 2024 presenting another challenging year for the cannabis sector as a result of rescheduling uncertainty, the REIT's performance was solid as AFFO saw a 10% increase year-over-year. They also raised their dividend 8% to $1.70, showing stability & resilience in their financials.

Total revenue also grew a solid 6% year-over-year to $50.1 million, driven by their May 2024 acquisition and rent escalators. However, this was partially offset by their two troubled tenants.

And for 2025, NLCP's management anticipates continued headwinds due to the DEA's decision regarding the rescheduling of cannabis.

But NewLake's management remain optimistic of a positive outcome. In my opinion, I also think cannabis has about a 60% - 75% chance of being rescheduled.

Rescheduling would also likely have a positive impact on their two troubled tenants, Revolutionary Clinics & Calypso's ability to pay rent, as this will alleviate certain costs and potentially increase cash flows.

But currently, NLCP has a plan in place for Revolutionary Clinics to continue paying 50% of contractual rent while the tenant works to restructure or sell the business. If they fail to be successful, NLCP will take ownership of the property and look to re-tenant.

With regard to Calypso, the REIT continues to use security deposits to cover the rent while they work to sort out issues and figure out a long-term solution.

While management remains optimistic, investors should keep an eye on both tenants' ability to pay rent. While I expect headwinds to remain over the short term, I'm looking to see a solution over the next 2 to 3 quarters before considering downgrading NewLake.

Looking to the positive, NLCP continued to deploy capital in 2024. They committed $445 million of capital across 15 facilities and 17 dispensaries in 12 states.

This represented 1.7 million square feet. For comparison, this was slightly higher than last year that saw management commit $428 million across 14 facilities and 17 dispensaries totaling 1.6 million square feet.

They also closed on a dispensary acquisition in Ohio with one of their larger tenants, Cresco (OTCQX:CRLBF), which makes up 12.9% of their portfolio.

Near 12% Yield Attractive

As previously mentioned, NLCP remains a riskier investment. But their 11.9% yield, valuation, and solid fundamentals remain attractive.

Although they raised the dividend 8% in 2024, I expect them to keep the dividend steady or raise it only $0.01 until the macro environmental picture becomes clearer.

And despite their AFFO payout ratio increasing from 78% to 82% year-over-year, this was still solid and in management's targeted range of 80% - 90%.

As you can see from the chart below, despite tenant issues, AFFO remained relatively stable and increased slightly from $0.51 to $0.52. For the year, this totaled $2.08, a 10% increase from 2023's $1.89.

While unsuspected volatility and future tenant issues could eventually lead to a dividend cut, I think NewLake's 11.9% yield is safe for the near to medium-term. However, their tenant struggles do warrant caution, prompting investors to keep an eye on this in the coming quarters.

Strong Financial Position

As mentioned in my previous article, their share repurchases, and low leverage do provide some flexibility regarding their ability to sustain their high yield.

For the year, management bought back roughly 585,000 shares. And going forward, I expect them to continue taking advantage of their discount to book value, which currently stands at just 0.78x.

Moreover, the REIT is well-positioned with only $8 million in total debt and enough cash on hand to satisfy this with $20.2 million.

They also had robust available liquidity on their revolver at $82.4 million, giving them a higher likelihood to continue funding the dividend as well as taking advantage of investment opportunities. Especially, if cannabis gets rescheduled.

They also don't have any leases expiring until 2029. And even then, these were minimal at only 1.7% and none after until 2031. So, from a fundamental standpoint, NewLake Capital remains in a comfortable position.

Double-Digit Upside Potential

While NewLake Capital offers solid upside to their price target of $20 a share, the stock is likely to trade rangebound unless cannabis gets successfully rescheduled.

If it is rescheduled, I expect NLCP to see capital appreciation. Until then, investors can enjoy a near 12% yield while they wait for more clarity in the industry.

Risks

Although I think downside is limited as headwinds regarding the rescheduling uncertainty are likely priced in, the REIT could see further downside should a recession come to fruition.

With now expected slower GDP growth and a deteriorating financial outlook, increased recession fears are warranted. If so, NewLake Capital's financials could become negatively impacted as tenants are likely to face further financial distress.

Bottom Line

Despite their troubled tenants, NewLake's financials remained resilient. Moreover, their low leverage and frequent buybacks enhance the safety of their near 12% yield, at least for the near to medium term.

Going forward, investors should remain cautious, especially if volatility regarding the economy picks up, as this could result of current and additional tenants facing financial distress.

While NewLake Capital Partners remains a riskier investment due to lingering issues with tenants, their double-digit yield, discount to book value, and solid payout ratio make them attractive for investors seeking income.
