data.title: WiseTech Global: All But Easygoing
data.description:
WiseTech Global Limited (OTCPK:WTCHF) (OTCPK:WIGBY) [WTC:AU] is currently navigating through rather difficult waters, due in particular to a number of corporate governance issues.

My investment thesis here is that the WiseTech shares are currently only a hold.

The company continues to grow strongly, albeit not as strongly as in previous years. However, what bothers me most is how much WiseTech is still too heavily geared towards CEO and founder Richard White. This recently led to mass resignations of independent directors in February 2025. Furthermore, new product launches have been delayed, although it is unclear whether this is related to the internet conditions at WiseTech. In my opinion, however, it is quite likely that there is a connection if the founder and CEO resigns from his role, only to return again.

In my view, the risks outweigh the opportunities, especially when held against the high valuation, so the stock only gets a ‘hold’ rating.

WiseTech in a nutshell

WiseTech is an Australian software company that provides logistics solutions worldwide. According to the company, 24 of the top 25 global freight forwarders and 46 of the top 50 global third-party logistics providers are customers. However, this only refers to WiseTech's main product, CargoWise.

However, CargoWise is also the most important solution offered by WiseTech. CargoWise acts as an operating system for logistics companies. It includes freight forwarding, customs clearance, transport and warehouse management, shipping, tracking, and cross-border compliance.

In addition, WiseTech has acquired a large number of other companies in recent years. You can find an overview of the different solutions here.

However, these individual businesses (grouped together as ‘non-CargoWise’ businesses) are not very growth-oriented overall and only account for a portion of total sales. The non-CargoWise businesses only generated sales of AUD 161.4m in FY 2024, compared to AUD 157.2m in FY 2023, which corresponds to a growth rate of only 3%.

CargoWise itself grew organically by 19% over the same period. However, WiseTech is also implementing many acquisitions into the CargoWise platform, so the total revenue of the CargoWise platform in 2024 increased by 33% to AUD 880.3m. Overall, revenue in FY 2024 was, therefore, AUD 1bn (FY 2023: AUD 816.8m).

All but easy-going

At first glance, CargoWise looks like an absolute dream compounder.

Revenues rose from AUD 102.8m to AUD 1bn between 2016 and 2024, which corresponds to an average annual growth rate of 33%.

Over the same period, adjusted EPS rose from AUD 0.06 to AUD 0.87, which corresponds to an annual growth rate of almost 40%.

CargoWise's success is based on the following three aspects, the so-called 3P (‘product, penetration, profitability’) strategy:

Firstly, we look to find fundamental flaws, operating problems, inefficient models and incomplete or ineffective processes, and then embed and automate improvements so that we revolutionize the industry's established model.

Secondly, because of the many cost and management efficiencies achieved through CargoWise, we're driving value for our customers across their entire cost base. This is through four layers of cost efficiencies, which start with eliminating legacy IT costs, then expands to lowering labor costs, reducing fines and penalties, and finally, optimizing freight costs.

Thirdly, these cost efficiencies and the operational simplicity CargoWise enables for our customers results in industry consolidation through M&A and the rapid, successful and profitable integration of the businesses they acquire whilst also driving competitive advantage and profitable organic growth. (Source: earnings call transcript 1H25)

Even though growth momentum has slowed, WiseTech continues to grow. Revenues in the first half of FY 2025 grew by 17%. The CargoWise platform organically increased revenues by 20% to a total of AUD 331.7m. EBIT rose by 31% with a gross profit margin increase of 2pp to 86%. WiseTech's rule of 40 is 50%. This is 9pp below the previous year's figure, but this is mainly due to one-off effects from the acquisitions of Envase and Blume in the same period last year.

The disproportionate increase in profitability is mainly due to the following cost-saving factors:

  • The share of product design and development expenses fell from 17% to 16%.
  • Sales and marketing expenses fell by AUD 2.7m and thus by 2pp from 8% to 6%.
  • General and administration expenses also fell by 1pp from 14% to 13% when adjusted for M&A costs.

In addition, WiseTech has recently invested heavily in the product pipeline around the so-called ‘3 breakthrough products’:

  • ComplianceWise was launched in Q1 FY 2025 and is designed to help companies minimize compliance risks within global supply chains.
  • CargoWise Next was also launched, and the systematic rollout is expected in the second half of FY 2025. This is a relaunch of the existing CargoWise architecture, which includes many new features.
  • The third will be the launch of CargoWise Container Transport Optimization, which focuses on container movements and is the result of the acquisition of Blume Global. WiseTech acquired Blume in 2023 for USD 414m.

So it could all be so easy for investors.

A company with a fairly high valuation of a P/E ratio north of 50 but with strong profitable growth and a large moat. A classic compounder.

If it weren't for CEO and founder Richard White. In October 2024, Richard White was forced to resign following allegations of inappropriate behavior. Even though an independent auditor ‘cleared him of wrongdoing,’ new allegations emerged against Richard White in February 2025.

The situation escalated when four independent directors resigned on 23 February 2025.

This even led to WiseTech breaking ASX listing rules. These rules require that the audit and risk committee have at least three non-executive directors. The topic was also touched on during the earnings call. In response to an analyst's question, Richard White replied:

First of all, the issues that you're referring to are a uniquely Australian issue. And not to be discounted, they have to be properly managed. ...

But in the customer's mind, the value that we create for them is very substantial.

And they have asked time and time again over the last six months, what does the future hold for the product? What does the future hold for you, Richard? Are we going to see the company continue its trajectory of being an innovator, a globally capable innovator that helps our business be better and better?

And the correct answer to that is we are working very hard for customers. They don't think about this market or these governance issues in Australia. They think about what is right for them, for their product, for their business and for the long term of WiseTech as it affects that business.

I don't want to make any judgment on these events, as I lack the insights to do so. However, it seems that Richard White is now firmly back in the highest executive chair after his resignation last year.

On the one hand, this, of course, ensures continuity, but on the other hand, the question arises as to whether the good governance mechanisms at WiseTech are still working properly.

And then there is another aspect that I, as an investor, consider to be equally important: the massive unrest in the company is affecting the guidance. Two announcements are striking here:

First, the launch of the three breakthrough products is to be postponed.

Secondly, management expects WiseTech to achieve only the lower end of the guidance range and thus 'only' 20% growth. Both items are connected, as the new revenue guidance is due to further delays to the rollout of the three breakthrough products

It is somewhat unclear whether the shifts are related to the change in the board composition. At least from my point of view, it seems understandable. WiseTech published the updated guidance on the same day and in the same ASX Announcement as it announced the change in the board composition. In addition, WiseTech mentioned a connection in another press release, at least with regard to the product launch of CargoWise Container Transport Optimization:

As a result of distractions flowing from the recent media attention and the organizational changes that have subsequently been implemented, the commercial launch of Container Transport Optimization has been delayed. It is now expected to launch in the second half of FY25, resulting in a delay to anticipated revenue. WiseTech considers it appropriate to update its FY25 guidance.

What investors get for their money

WiseTech is traditionally valued quite expensively. The current adjusted P/E ratio is 80, which is slightly below the historical average of the last few years of 106.

In my view, historical multiples are no longer appropriate at present. For one thing, the leadership at WiseTech is still unclear. The turmoil has also scared off other investors. The stock has been trading at a significant discount since February, although it was previously trading near all-time highs.

Regardless of the events surrounding Richard White, it should be noted that the growth momentum is slowing. Although the current cost-cutting plan is benefiting the bottom line, in the long term the market will focus primarily on the growth and success of breakthrough products.

I honestly find it difficult to accept an adjusted P/E ratio higher than 50. On the basis of such a multiple, WiseTech is still significantly overvalued, with a downside potential of almost 40%.

The dividend yield of just 0.24% is a drop in the ocean, even though the interim dividend was recently increased by 31%.

Conclusion

The internal shake-up at WiseTech could have a cleansing effect so that the company is now focusing more on the product side again. Here, the three breakthrough products could give the company another growth spurt. Nevertheless, the governance issues continue to cast a shadow over WiseTech. Even if Richard White assumes that customers attach little importance to this and tend to look at the products and how they help their own business, it is now the case that events are also affecting the introduction of new products, which is directly related to customer relations.

In short, in a few years' time, people may look back and say that WiseTech offered investors a perfect investment opportunity in April 2025.

That may be.

However, my primary concern is to avoid unnecessary risks.

In my view, with an adjusted P/E ratio of 80 and the governance issues discussed, as well as the associated product delays, WiseTech offers an unfavorable ratio of growth prospects to the associated share price potential. In my view, the stock is, therefore, only a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fear Creates Fortunes: Grab These 7 Blue-Chip Bargains Today
data.description:
Correction Update: Fighting Fear With Facts

S&P PEGY (Cash-Adjusted PE/ (Yield + Growth) Valuation Profile:

S&P 10-Year Median Yield 1.77%
S&P 10-year CAGR FCF/Share Growth 9.60%
S&P 10-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.11
S&P 17-Year Median Yield 1.88%
S&P 17-Year FCF/Share Growth 8.22%
S&P 17-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.17
S&P Current Yield 1.42%
S&P LT Growth Consensus: 11.00%
S&P Current PEGY Ratio 1.10
S&P PEGY Overvaluation -0.8%
Upside To Fair Value 0.77%
12-Month Forward EPS Growth 12.4%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified Total Return Potential 14.6%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified S&P 6,366.61

(Source: Dividend Kings S&P Valuation Tool, FactSet)

History says the market will likely be up 20% to 25% in the next 12 months.

Fundamentals would justify a 14.6% gain in the next year.

Market Sentiment Is 12th Percentile Bearish!

Investor sentiment, according to Goldman's proprietary sentiment model, is 1.2 standard deviations bearish, which has only occurred 11.5% of the time since 2009.

In other words, 7/8th of the time investors were more bullish than now.

Consumer Sentiment Is Terrible (Understandable But Also Ridiculous)

Household sentiment about their finances in the next year is worse than during the Pandemic and the Great Recession.

  • I understand that a lot of people are legitimately worried about their future.
  • My family is too.

Keep in mind that during the Pandemic, we lost 22 million jobs in two months (10 years' worth of job growth), and during the GFC, we hit 10% unemployment, and the stock market fell 58%.

I think it's tragic that so many Americans are saying they are this terrified for their future.

This Is Likely What Consumers Are Worried About

And no one indeed wins in a trade war.

This is per 10% tariffs, and FactSet reports it might be twice as bad.

Heading into last week, multiple reports noted that Trump's 2-Apr tariff announcement would be narrower than initially planned, excluding some nations and sectoral-specific tariffs. These reports helped drive a big rally in US equities on 24-Mar with all of the major indexes up over 1.4% to start the week. While Trump also talked about more lenient reciprocal tariffs last week, this took a backseat to the announcement of "permanent" additional 25% tariffs on autos not made in the US. In addition, there were several negative tariff headlines out this weekend (Washington Post, NBC News, Politico). These highlighted the potential for an across-the-board hike of up to 20%, $600B in additional tariffs per year, or $6T over next decade (which would represent largest peacetime tax hike in modern history), and tariffs on all countries (not just the "dirty 15" flagged by Bessent). The other big concern has revolved around the potential for a rocky rollout and lack of clarity that exacerbates a Trump 2.0 policy uncertainty dynamic that has increasingly spilled over to the underlying economy." - FactSet Research Terminal (emphasis original)

As FactSet points out, "Consumers should be concerned about the potential for a $6 trillion tax hike, the largest in peace-time history."

I'm not saying if this is good or bad. Just telling you the facts.

But 4% long-term inflation? 5% in the next year?

For now, everything is only potential impacts to fundamentals.

Real-Time Economic Data: What The Economy Looks like Today

This is what the media is focusing on. It spins a "recession is here!" narrative that "feels" so right.

  • Double dip "Vibesession"

Here's what the media won't talk about.

The NY Fed thinks the economy will most likely be growing at 2.6% through the end of June.

The St. Louis Fed thinks we're growing at 2.3%.

The Dallas Fed, using the most up-to-date daily and weekly data, estimates real-time GDP growth of 2.1%, modestly lower than the 2.4% we were at when the tariffs went up on Feb. 4.

Real-Time Inflation

Consumers aren't the only ones expecting higher inflation this year.

And here's what's actually happening, courtesy of 18 million daily prices from 85 data partners at Truflation.

So what is inflation? If economists think 3% is coming (3.5% from Goldman Sachs) and consumers 5%?

A year ago, inflation was 3%.

Inflation hit 1.3% a few weeks ago, that was the bottom for the year, and is currently 1.76%.

As you can see in the above chart, the day tariffs first began, on Feb. 4, real-time inflation was 2.19%.

Inflation has, so far, fallen during the trade war.

  • So far, consumers and economists are wrong about inflation.
  • Also about recession risk.

Recession odds are about 40% according to betting markets, similar to JPMorgan's 40% estimate.

The most bearish estimates for recession risk are from the commodity and bond markets.

The big institutional investors (like hedge funds, pension funds, endowments, family offices, and sovereign wealth funds) are currently hedging as is there is a 60% risk of recession.

  • The range on recession hedges has ranged from 39% to 68% in the last two weeks.

Remember, a pension fund has to hedge its stock portfolio and so the fact that bond and commodity markets are pricing in about 60% recession risk is actually not as scary as it sounds.

  • It would be 100% if the economic data were plunging anywhere as quickly as sentiment surveys.

Buy Something Smart: Blue Chips Trading At 52-Week Lows

I keep telling my readers and Dividend Kings Subscribers to "buy something smart, and you'll thank me in a year."

That's because historically, sentiment this bearish and volatility spikes like these trigger strong 20% to 25% rallies within a year.

15% of that would be justified by fundamentals.

But it's also a market of stocks, not a stock market.

Largest Companies Trading At 52-Week Lows

There are so many companies trading in bear markets right now.

About 12% of stocks are in a bear market at any given time. Right now, it's about 25%.

Dozens of companies (about 150 actually) are trading at 52-week lows.

So here are my favorite recommendations to consider.

Blue-Chip Bargains At 52-Week Lows That You Don't Want To Miss

These seven companies average a 2.4% yield but are 24% historically undervalued (S&P is fairly valued).

Their average quality is Ultra SWAN, wide moat aristocrat level quality.

Their average 12-month return potential (justified by fundamentals) is an astonishing 36%.

  • 50% more than the S&P's historical 12-month post-correction gain.

Buffett-Like Total Return Potential From Blue-Chip Bargains Hiding In Plain Sight

Target (TGT)

PPG Industries (PPG)

NIKE (NKE)

CSX (CSX)

J.B. Hunt (JBHT)

Agilent Tech (A)

Omnicom (OMC)

Risk Profile: What Might Go Wrong In The Next Few Months

When the facts change, I change my mind. What do you do sir?" John Maynard Keynes

Right now, the economy looks strong, and the impact of the trade war is almost entirely on sentiment and not on hard data... yet.

But things might get worse, a lot worse.

Royal Bank of Canada is warning that, if the S&P breaks below the 5500 lows set on March 13, we might see a -20% non-recessionary bear market.

Just like the 20% bear markets we had since 2009.

You might have forgotten that the booming market (with 11X S&P gains from 2009 through 2025) included plenty of bear markets.

And of those four bear markets, only one included a recession, which lasted two months.

Three non-recessionary bear markets in the last 14 years.

I'm not saying these blue-chip bargains are bottomed, I'm saying that anyone buying them right now is "buying something smart, and will likely thank me in a year."

That's not a promise; it's a high probability bet that the market will be much higher (as it usually is after a correction like this), and these blue-chip bargains will likely be up even more.

Bottom Line: Don't Let Trade War Fears Keep You From Minting Money In The Next Year

I'm not a market timer.

I can only tell you what the fundamentals say (stocks are about 1% undervalued and offer about 15% upside potential in the next year) and the short-term probability curves.

But what happens in the next week, month, or quarter is meaningless to your long-term financial goals.

Think about it.

$1 invested in US stocks in 1802 would be worth $2.356 million today, 7% inflation-adjusted returns for centuries.

Through a civil war.

Through a Flu pandemic that killed 1 in 20 Americans.

Through two world wars and the worst depression in human history.

Through a cold war, numerous regular wars, and the biggest stock market bubble in history (tech bubble).

Through a Great Financial Crisis that resulted in the second-biggest market crash ever.

Through a Pandemic that wiped out 10 years of job growth in two months and resulted in -8.5% GDP growth (-33% annualized, the worst ever recorded).

Through not one, not two, but three periods of sky-high inflation.

  • 2022, 1970s and post-World War II (22% highest inflation ever recorded)

Through interest rates as high as 20%.

In other words, unless you think things will be worse than all that in the future, don't worry about whether the market is 1% from the bottom, has already hit bottom, or might fall as much as 15% more.

In the GFC, Buffett wrote his "Buy American: I Am" Article on October 16th. The market was down 38%, slightly more than the historical 36% recessionary bear market average.

He didn't predict this was the bottom; he just said long-term investors would regret not buying at these prices.

  • The equivalent of my "Buy something smart, and you'll thank me in a year;)"

Guess what happened next? There's a reason they call him the Oracle of Omaha, and it's not because of his ability to time market bottoms.

Stocks fell another 32% right after Buffett pounded the table with his NY Times op-Ed.

Buffett was dead wrong! Except he wasn't.

One year after his table-pounding call, the market was up 19%, and the Nasdaq 33%, despite falling 32% right after Buffett "was wrong."

Can you see that 32% decline? While traders care about 32% corrections, long-term investors are shooting for hundreds or even thousands of percentage gains.

Life-changing returns are earned in scary times like these.

Remember, everyone says they will be "greedy when others are fearful" the next time there is a correction.

Except there are ALWAYS scary headlines that make you hesitate to pull the trigger.

When stocks are climbing the wall of worry and at record highs, there are always smart-sounding reasons to sell.

When stocks are in a correction (92% of the time, they are not at record highs), there are always reasons not to buy.

But whether you're an index investor (the S&P is fairly valued, and fundamentals justify a 15% return in the next year) or a stock picker, the time to buy something smart is always.

And especially today!

Life is infinitely more complex than you can imagine. But living well is much simpler than you think. - Adam Galas

Buying these blue-chip bargains today is buying something smart. Whether it takes a year for you to feel like Buffett, or a few years, the point is that disciplined financial science, focusing on safety and quality first, and prudent valuation and sound risk management always, is how you make your own luck on Wall Street.

data.title: The YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF: Recent Sell-Off Shows Limits To The Fund's Structure
data.description:
Often times, adverse market conditions inform investors more about their holdings in a short period of time than the longer-term performance in good times tells them about the same holdings. A good investment should withstand the test of time, and an extended sell-off is sometimes a good way to stress test the viability of financial options.

Tesla (TSLA) has been one of the most volatile stocks in the market for some time, and this company's price moves have become even more unpredictable recently as Musk and brands associated with him have been politicized. There are also growing signs of an economic slowdown and some heightened uncertainty surrounding Trump's use of tariffs as well.

One fund that seeks to leverage the volatility levels in Tesla is the YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY) This YieldMax fund uses a synthetic options strategy, where the managers are long the stock with call spreads while also selling puts and owning conservative income-producing assets such as Treasury Bonds.

TSLY has offered investors total returns of negative 1.02 percent since the fund's inception date in November 2022. Tesla has offered investors total returns of 46.54 percent during this same time period.

I last covered TSLY in August of 2024, and I rated the fund a sell. Today, I am reiterating my sell rating of this YieldMax fund. TSLY has only slightly outperformed Tesla during the extended recent sell-off, and this ETF has significantly and consistently underperformed the volatile stock during rallies for several years. This fund is likely too volatile for most income investors in addition to being an inferior option for growth investors, since the ETF should continue to underperform Tesla during better times.

TSLY has an expense ratio of 1.04 percent, nearly 893 million in assets under management, and a 30-day SEC yield of 3.87 percent. This fund's core holdings are call options and US treasury bonds.

TSLY purchases at-the-money call options and then sells call options up to 15 percent out of the money. The term of these options is 1 to 6 months. The fund also sells put options to generate income to buy the call spreads, in addition to holding treasury bonds essentially as collateral for the synthetic long position. The ETF makes monthly payouts, but these payments have been very unpredictable primarily because of how much the implied levels of volatility with the options TSLY sells can vary even month-to-month.

TSLY has paid out monthly income since the fund's inception, ranging from a high of $2.13 a share in July of 2023, to a low of nearly $.46 a share in March of 2025. While the recent drop in implied volatility in Tesla that has caused payments to be lower is not likely sustainable, TSLY's payouts should continue to vary widely. The fund's payouts are also taxed at ordinary income rates, but some of the money returned is classified as a return on capital that can lower an investor's cost basis.

The core problem with TSLY is that the fund's options strategy of capping the upside potential while leaving all of the downside risk exposed offers a bad risk-reward profile to investors. While TSLY outperforms Tesla slightly during sell-offs, this fund has significantly and consistently underperformed the carmaker this ETF is long during rallies. TSLY uses call spreads where the fund sells monthly options that are usually only 1 to 15 percent out of the money, and because of the significant moves in short periods of time in Tesla, this ETF does not capture all of the upward moves in the underlying company. Volatility levels in both Tesla and the overall market are also likely to remain further elevated moving forward because of multiple reasons. TSLY should continue to underperform Tesla at a higher rate in the current more unpredictable market environment.

TSLY has only offered investors total returns of 3 percent better than Tesla during the sell-off in the company over the last three months.

Elon Musk and Tesla are both politicized companies now, and recent economic data has shown that the risks of a slowdown are increasing, with consumer spending numbers disappointing in the last couple of months. Trump's use of tariffs also brings more uncertainty to an already volatile overall market environment. Tesla's stock is likely to continue to see exaggerated moves for some time.

The idea of generating significant income in a short period of time obviously sounds favorable. Still, the recent sell-off highlighted the flaws in TSLY's risk-reward profile. While some more aggressive income investors may find this fund appealing, most individuals focused on growth and regular payouts should be able to find better options.

data.title: Zevra Therapeutics: Mixed Feelings On Miplyffa Approval
data.description:
I covered Zevra Therapeutics (ZVRA) in May 2023, when I said I was not convinced by our old KemPharm (previous avatar of Zevra) repurposing an old molecule, arimoclomol, and running clinical trials in a rare disease called Niemann-Pick type C disease ("NPC"). My doubts about the molecule stemmed from its multiple trial and regulatory failures before, in ALS and NPC respectively; my doubts about Zevra arose from my long-term reservations about repurposed companies.

However, in September 2024, the FDA approved arimoclomol (now branded Miplyffa) for Niemann-Pick type C disease after an advisory panel voted 11 to 5 attesting to its efficacy in NPC. The condition has about a thousand patients and no approved therapies in the US before 2024. Another research claims there are 943 patients in the US. However that may be, there is no doubt that NPC is an ultra-rare condition, an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder where the body fails to transport lipids within cells, leading to their accumulation in vital organs including the brain, and general organ dysfunction.

On September 20, 2024, the FDA approved arimoclomol, and on September 24, 2024, just 4 days later, the FDA approved Aqneursa (levacetylleucine), developed by privately held IntraBio. Other therapies in development for this rare disease are Trappsol® Cyclo™ (Hydroxypropyl-beta-cyclodextrin), being developed by Cyclo Therapeutics, and AZ-3102, from Azafaros A.G. Miglustat from JNJ is approved ex-US, but it was never approved here. According to one source:

Miglustat (Opfolda, Yargesa, Zavesca) has been approved in the European Union, Canada, and Japan and is considered a standard of care for treating progressive neurological complications in NPC internationally.

Being that NPC is a mostly pediatric disease (median age of death is 13 years (range, 0.1 to 69 years)), the FDA must have been under some pressure to approve an NPC drug. It went ahead and approved two of them.

Aqneursa is not exactly a direct competitor of Miplyffa; rather, their actions are complementary. While Miplyffa targets NPC progression at a molecular level, Aqneursa focuses more on symptom relief, particularly neurological function. Data:

The mean (±SD) change from baseline in the SARA [Scale for the Assessment and Rating of Ataxia] total score was −1.97±2.43 points after 12 weeks of receiving NALL and −0.60±2.39 points after 12 weeks of receiving placebo (least-squares mean difference, −1.28 points; 95% confidence interval, −1.91 to −0.65; P<0.001).

Miplyffa was approved on the basis of a different endpoint:

A 0.2-point decrease on the R4DNPCCSS was observed in patients who received arimoclomol (Miplyffa) in combination with miglustat, compared with an increase of 1.9 points in patients who received placebo with miglustat.

Interestingly, SARA was a secondary endpoint in the Miplyffa trial, and was found not to be statistically significant.

Aqneursa is also given to slightly older people; in the trial, patients 4 years or older were chosen. It is an oral solution, which may be easier to deliver to young children than Miplyffa, which is an oral capsule. Both have similar safety profiles:

Miplyffa safety:

Adverse events occurred in 30/34 patients (88.2%) receiving arimoclomol and 12/16 (75.0%) receiving placebo. Fewer patients had serious adverse events with arimoclomol (5/34, 14.7%) vs placebo (5/16, 31.3%). Treatment-related serious adverse events (n = 2) included urticaria and angioedema.

Aqneursa safety:

A total of 79 adverse events occurred in 36 patients when they were receiving NALL, and 75 events occurred in 30 patients when they were receiving placebo. No adverse events led to premature discontinuation of the trial. No adverse events occurred in more than 10% of patients when they were receiving NALL. Three patients each had 1 adverse event that was assessed by the investigator to be related to NALL (anal incontinence, restless legs, and rosacea). These events were all transient. The incidence of upper respiratory tract infection was higher when the patients were receiving NALL (10%) than when they were receiving placebo (5%). The incidence of falls was lower when the patients were receiving NALL (7%) than when they were receiving placebo (15%). Epilepsy, which is a feature of Niemann–Pick disease type C, occurred once when a patient was receiving NALL.

Thus, the profiles were slightly different, with urticaria and angioedema occurring as SAEs in Miplyffa, while AEs (not SAEs) that occurred with Aqneursa were anal incontinence, restless legs, and rosacea. Overall, both were safe and well-tolerated drugs, hence approved in a pediatric population. However, the two medications are not to be used together.

The above source also points out things I had been wary about in my earlier coverage. It could well be that the FDA was in a hurry to approve this molecule, given the following observations:

While the primary outcome was assessed via a validated assessment tool, the quality of evidence is considered low as there are several uncertainties that remain including lack of additional well designed confirmatory trials, lack of a well-established MOA, unknown effectiveness without miglustat, and a small population size that could impact the interpretability of the rescored R4NPCCSS which limit the durability of results. Although arimoclomol (Miplyffa) showed a statistically significant difference in the modified R4DNPCCSS score, the effect of treatment was relatively small.

In relation to the use of miglustat, note the following for Aqneursa:

Most participants continued to receive background miglustat throughout the trial. Although the FDA label does not mandate the concurrent administration of miglustat with levacetylleucine (Aqneursa), it is probable that healthcare providers will choose to continue miglustat therapy when prescribing levacetylleucine (Aqneursa) as a majority of participants in the pivotal clinical trial were on concomitant miglustat (85%).

So miglustat is not approved in the US, but the FDA has approved a medicine only when used in conjunction with miglustat; and while that is not the case with the other molecule, 85% of the trial population were on miglustat, so most physicians are going to play it safe and use miglustat anyway. What a strange sort of approval.

I should also note some of the reactions of the members of the adcomm, as I read on FiercePharma:

Despite the overall favorable vote, many of the panel members were torn on the medicines’ efficacy package.

“I voted yes but it was a very reluctant yes,” private consultant Jonathan Mink, M.D., Ph.D., said during the meeting. “I found the effect size to be small and the strength of the data to be weak, but overall the bulk of the data favored a slightly positive effect.”

Many panelists pointed to the high unmet need in NPC in justifying their "yes" votes.

More on the adcomm panels’ voting decisions can be found here. From my reading, it was a vote to support a high unmet need, and the sad condition many patient families find themselves in. But it was good business, nevertheless, for a small company.

According to Endpoints news, “Miplyffa’s wholesale acquisition cost would range between $40,000 and $106,000 per month, depending on dosage.” The company also received a priority review voucher along with the approval, which they sold for $150mn. Interestingly, Endpoint also says that “William Blair analyst Lachlan Hanbury-Brown estimates that the US patient population is about 300 to 350 people.”

Post launch updates

Zevra made Miplyffa available by November, while Aqneursa was immediately available post-approval; in their latest earnings release, Zevra said the following:

As of Dec. 31, 2024, Zevra received 109 total prescription enrollment forms for Miplyffa, including all [90] active U.S. Expanded Access Program [EAP] participants. To date in this early phase of the launch, patients have received payor authorization through direct formulary coverage or via the medical exception process as Zevra’s market access team continues to engage with payors to formalize coverage.

Thus, background miglustat or not, Miplyffa is well on to a good start, and patients in the US who had no other recourse but to take miglustat off-label (it is approved in the US for Gaucher Disease) now have an FDA-approved way of taking miglustat. The uptake has been impressive; as FiercePharma noted:

In five weeks at the end of the year, the therapy drummed up $10.1 million in net revenue, the company reported. The result blew Cantor Fitzgerald’s sales estimate of $5.2 million out of the water, the analysts wrote in a recent note to clients.

Zebra has another approved and marketed asset, the urea cycle disorder drug Olpruva, which has a small revenue stream. The company is running a trial for celiprolol for the treatment of Vascular Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome [VEDS], where it is enrolling patients.

Financials

ZVRA has a market cap of $396mn and a cash balance of $75.5 million, plus the $148.3mn it will receive from the sale of the PRV. Thus, they have $223.3mn in pro forma cash. Last quarter, their Cost of Goods Sold was $13.7 million, which includes non-cash components. Net revenue was “$23.6 million, comprised of $10.1 million in Miplyffa net revenue, $0.1 million in OLPRUVA net revenue, $9.1 million in net reimbursements from the French expanded access program [EAP] for arimoclomol, and $4.3 million in royalties and other reimbursements under the AZSTARYS® license agreement.” R&D expenses were $42.1 million for FY 2024, while SG&A expenses were $54.9 million. Their CFO was ($16.3mn), and with no capex, their FCF was -$16.3mn. That gives them a cash runway of 13+ quarters.

About their cash runway, the company guides as follows:

Given our execution to date on the Miplyffa and OLPRUVA commercial launches and the sale of the PRV, our Form 10-K will not include a Going Concern disclosure. Based on our current operating forecast, existing resources extend our cash runway into 2029. The cash runway forecast does not include proceeds from the sale of the PRV, but it does include anticipated net revenue from Miplyffa and OLPRUVA sales, net reimbursements from the French EAP for arimoclomol, royalties under the AZSTARYS license agreement, and continued investments into our development pipeline.

Interestingly, this guidance does not include the PRV funds, but does include anticipated funds.

Risks

ZVRA as a company will do quite well, it appears, at least until the differences between Miplyffa and Aqneursa, if any exist, become more prominent. In my opinion, Miplyffa is a way for US patients to access miglustat - what the status of insurance will be on that issue is an interesting question. Broadly, while my sympathies are with NPC patients, and I see that this company could well make some money here, I, along with some members of the adcomm, believe there is still an unmet need here. That is the key risk with ZVRA.

Bottom line

ZVRA looks like a decent investment now, but from a long-term perspective, and given the still remaining unmet need I cited, I will avoid this stock.
